lens.included_activities: U.S. operating full-service restaurant firms in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide repeat, seated food-and-beverage service to external guests, including independent and franchised operators when control of the operating company is transferable.
lens.excluded_activities: Firms outside the EBITDA band, non-operating shells, captive internal dining units, passive non-control interests, and establishments whose primary model belongs in limited-service restaurants, bars, catering, institutional food service, or another NAICS industry.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers pay posted menu prices per visit for prepared food, beverages, table service, and the dining experience; repeat use is frequent but generally non-contractual, with some additional delivery, takeout, events, and loyalty-program revenue.

a.bridge: The BLS exact-industry occupation table supplies employment and mean wages, while O*NET supplies manager tasks and BLS identifies technology-driven changes for waiters and hosts. I assigned task-level exposure judgments within the major occupations and weighted them by employment times mean wage. This is not a measured AI-exposure share and likely biases upward if socially nuanced service and exception handling prove harder to substitute, while omitting informal owner labor biases downward.
a.rationale: About 91% of industry employment is food-preparation and serving work, led by waiters at 32.12% and restaurant cooks at 19.75%. Most cooking, carrying, cleaning, sensory quality control, and live hospitality remain physical. The exposed portion is concentrated in ordering and payment, reservations, scheduling, recruiting, marketing, payroll, recordkeeping, inventory analysis, menu analysis, and routine customer communications; the estimate excludes technology already in use.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across independent and multi-unit full-service restaurants that maps paid hours and wages to tasks, identifies tools already deployed, and tests task completion by current restaurant AI/POS systems would materially change this range.
caveats: The latest reachable static six-digit OEWS table is May 2023 and excludes self-employed workers.
Occupation-level task descriptions do not reveal time shares or which tasks are already automated.
Tips and owner labor are imperfectly represented by OEWS wage measures, affecting wage weights.

rho.bridge: Restaurant surveys measure operator adoption and investment intentions, not the fraction of exposed labor opportunity implemented within five years. I use current 28% full-service AI adoption, reported use cases, and 2024 investment intentions as the starting point, then allow five years for diffusion. Intentions can overstate completed deployment, while the one-year survey horizon can understate five-year diffusion; workflow integration, staff adoption, guest preferences, and food-safety accountability pull implementation below technical exposure.
rho.rationale: In 2026, 28% of full-service operators reported using AI tools, but 94% of operators said technology investments over the prior two to three years had not permanently eliminated jobs. Earlier full-service investment intentions were much higher for back office, inventory, payment, and labor-management systems than for AI integration or robotics. Implementation should therefore reach a meaningful share of exposed administrative and transactional work but remain partial in high-touch dining and physical production.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of full-service restaurants reporting baseline and five-year labor hours by workflow, go-live dates, realized hours avoided, error rates, guest satisfaction, and abandoned deployments would materially move rho.
caveats: National Restaurant Association surveys mix firm sizes and may overrepresent operators engaged with the trade group.
Reported use of AI does not establish depth, persistence, or labor substitution.
The five-year technology frontier is unusually uncertain.

e.rationale: The frozen firm count already targets the EBITDA band, and an operating full-service restaurant normally serves external, repeat customers. Most such firms therefore fit the lens. The discount removes businesses whose economics are inseparable from a celebrity chef or owner, whose leases or franchise rights cannot be assigned on acceptable terms, whose records cannot support normalized earnings, or whose apparent EBITDA is not attached to a transferable operating platform.
e.change_evidence: A representative broker and lender file review of full-service restaurant firms with $1-10M normalized EBITDA, coding franchise transfer consent, lease assignability, owner dependence, financial-record quality, licensing, and buyer rejection reasons, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source directly measures eligibility under this bespoke lens.
The frozen count is margin-bridged from receipts and may include firms whose true normalized EBITDA lies outside the band.
Eligibility can vary sharply between scalable multi-unit concepts and chef-led single-location businesses.

s5.bridge: SBA evidence covers employer businesses and the broad accommodation-and-food-services sector, while BizBuySell covers disclosed restaurant transactions of all formats and mostly much smaller businesses. I translate the 64.5% all-business lifetime intention to sell, the sector's 38% purchased-origin share, and observed restaurant marketplace turnover into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability. The marketplace count biases downward by omitting private and larger deals but upward relative to the LMM lens because its sold restaurant median revenue is only $718,271 and many listings are owner-operator assets rather than transferable platforms.
s5.rationale: Restaurants have an established ownership-transfer channel: accommodation and food-services owners were more likely than most sectors to have purchased their firms, and BizBuySell reports 2,516 restaurant sales in 2025. However, a sale intention is not a five-year event, listings are not all qualifying control transfers, and the observed marketplace skews far below the target EBITDA band. A roughly one-in-four five-year probability is therefore a bounded proxy rather than an observed rate.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a), and business-broker records matched to a denominator of U.S. full-service restaurant firms with $1-10M normalized EBITDA, with recapitalizations, asset liquidations, internal reorganizations, and failed deals removed, would materially change s5.
caveats: The evidence does not provide a clean LMM full-service-firm denominator.
Stated plans to sell have no specified timing and may never produce a transaction.
Restaurant marketplace transactions disproportionately represent small owner-operated businesses and asset sales.

q.rationale: Menu-price billing has no contractual cost-plus pass-through, so an operator initially keeps labor savings. Retention erodes quickly because customers re-shop on every visit, competing restaurants can adopt similar tools, and operators may share savings through value offers, service upgrades, or smaller price increases. Persistent margin pressure creates incentive to retain savings, but the 2026 industry summary reports that 42% of operators were not profitable in 2025 and consumers remain budget constrained, making neither full retention nor full pass-through plausible.
q.change_evidence: A five-year matched panel of full-service restaurants and local competitors linking verified automation savings to menu-price changes, portion and service quality, traffic, wages, and restaurant-level EBITDA would materially change q.
caveats: No public study isolates the pass-through of AI-derived labor savings in full-service restaurants.
Local competition, cuisine, alcohol mix, and concept differentiation create wide dispersion.
This range excludes volume effects, which belong in demand primitives, and implementation failure, which belongs in rho.

d5.bridge: BLS directly forecasts exact-industry real output from $452.7 billion in 2024 to $550.2 billion in 2034, a 2.0% compound annual rate. Compounding that rate for five years gives about 1.10, used as the base. Chained-dollar output is the closest public quantity proxy but is not a constant-quality count of the current service basket; menu mix, quality, off-premises sales, and new concepts can move measured output without proportional current-service quantity, so error can run in either direction.
d5.rationale: The exact-industry BLS forecast implies moderate real growth rather than contraction. The 2026 Restaurant Association outlook is directionally consistent, forecasting 1.3% real restaurant-industry sales growth for that year, while also flagging uneven traffic and consumer budget pressure. The range allows a mild five-year quantity decline or stronger population- and income-supported growth.
d5.change_evidence: Updated BLS exact-industry output projections plus a five-year forecast of full-service traffic or transactions at fixed menu mix and service quality from Circana, Technomic, Black Box Intelligence, or a comparable panel would materially change d5.
caveats: The BLS forecast starts in 2024 rather than the July 2026 run date and spans ten years rather than five.
The Restaurant Association outlook covers the broader restaurant industry, not only NAICS 722511.
Recession exposure and shifts toward grocery, delivery, or limited-service dining widen the downside.

o.bridge: BLS forecasts exact-industry output rising 21.5% and employment rising 2.5% from 2024 to 2034, and separately expects technology to reduce waiter and host intensity. The Restaurant Association reports that 68% of full-service operators expect technology to augment rather than replace labor. These measures do not observe the share of service quantity requiring an accountable restaurant operator; I infer high operator persistence because food production, safety, premises, alcohol compliance, service recovery, and hospitality remain physical accountabilities. The proxy may overstate o if delivery, self-service, or automated formats replace full-service occasions faster than forecast.
o.rationale: Ordering, payment, seating assistance, and some management tasks can be self-served or automated, but the current full-service basket still requires an entity accountable for procuring, preparing, serving, and safely handling food in a physical venue. BLS explicitly anticipates technology-enabled productivity while still forecasting industry employment growth, supporting limited displacement of the operator rather than elimination of the service model.
o.change_evidence: A consumer-occasion panel tracking migration from full-service dining to software-only, automated, grocery, meal-kit, and self-service substitutes, paired with establishment-level automation and staffing data, would materially change o.
caveats: Employment persistence is not the same as operator-required quantity.
Operator survey beliefs may lag technical change and are not five-year realized outcomes.
The estimate treats technology-assisted restaurants as still operator-required, consistent with the primitive definition.

narrative.executive_view: Full-service restaurants combine a large labor bill with many repeat consumer transactions, but most labor sits in physical cooking, carrying, cleaning, and live hospitality. The credible AI opportunity is therefore a bounded layer of administrative, analytical, ordering, payment, reservation, scheduling, recruiting, and service-support work rather than wholesale labor replacement. Demand is expected to grow modestly, while intense local competition and fragile margins constrain durable capture.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can forecast traffic and labor needs, draft schedules and marketing, answer routine inquiries, optimize menus and inventory, screen applicants, summarize operating data, and support ordering and payment. Connected restaurant systems are already moving into these workflows. Kitchen production, sensory judgment, food safety, service recovery, and the social experience remain difficult to substitute; adoption evidence also shows augmentation far more often than permanent job removal.
narrative.value_capture: Savings arrive first as restaurant-level margin because guests pay menu prices rather than reimbursing costs. Over five years, competitors can adopt the same tools and customers can respond to price and service differences on every visit, forcing some benefit into value offers, better service, wages, or lower menu-price growth. Differentiated concepts can retain more than commodity, price-sensitive restaurants.
narrative.firm_availability: Operating firms in the target EBITDA band usually satisfy the repeat external-customer lens, but transferability is reduced by chef or owner dependence, weak records, lease and franchise consents, and location-specific goodwill. Sector ownership is demonstrably purchased as well as founded, and restaurant sales are frequent in the small-business marketplace, but public evidence does not isolate qualifying LMM control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects exact-industry real output to grow 2.0% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 10% growth over five years if that path holds. The service remains operator-required because meals, safety, premises, licensing, and hospitality are physical accountabilities. The principal demand risks are recession sensitivity, consumer budget pressure, and substitution toward grocery, limited-service, delivery, or self-service occasions.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of a measured wage-weighted task-exposure study, the gap between stated technology use and verified labor hours avoided, and the lack of a clean transfer-rate denominator for $1-10M EBITDA full-service firms. Restaurant margins, traffic, cuisine mix, alcohol exposure, local wage rules, leases, and brand strength vary widely. Technology can also degrade hospitality or shift work rather than remove it.
narrative.principal_driver: A very large labor base contains repeatable administrative and transaction workflows that connected AI and restaurant software can streamline.
narrative.principal_weakness: Core production and hospitality are physical, while easy technology diffuses quickly through a fiercely competitive, low-margin market.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 722511 Full-Service Restaurants
sources.supports: Exact-industry occupation mix and wages: 5,289,580 jobs; 91.30% in food preparation and serving; waiters 32.12%, restaurant cooks 19.75%, supervisors 5.57%, dishwashers 5.79%, hosts 6.79%, and food-service managers 1.64%.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Food Service Managers
sources.supports: Manager task content including scheduling, payroll and budgets, inventory, purchasing, compliance records, reservations, staffing, complaints, menu analysis, and physical service and quality-control duties.

sources.title: BLS Factors Affecting Occupational Utilization, Projected 2024-34
sources.supports: For NAICS 722511, BLS expects technology-enabled self-service ordering and payment to reduce waiter intensity and robots to assist hosts, while other occupational changes reflect substitution and changing service formats.

sources.title: National Restaurant Association Restaurant Technology Landscape Report 2024
sources.supports: Full-service investment intentions: 52% back office, 53% inventory, 41% contactless ordering/payment, 36% labor management, 20% self-order/pay, 15% AI integration, and 7% robotics; 68% expected technology to augment rather than replace labor.

sources.title: National Restaurant Association Research Insight: Hiring and Staffing Report 2026
sources.supports: Twenty-eight percent of full-service operators reported using AI; among restaurant AI users, cited full-service use cases included marketing 66%, administrative tasks 41%, menu optimization 28%, ordering 23%, scheduling 21%, and reservations 32%; 94% of operators reported no permanent job elimination from recent technology investment.

sources.title: SBA Office of Advocacy Small Business Facts: Paths to Business Ownership
sources.supports: Accommodation and food-services owners were more likely to have purchased their firms at 38%; 64.5% of current owners across businesses reported plans to sell, without a specified timing horizon.

sources.title: BizBuySell Guide to Selling a Restaurant Business
sources.supports: BizBuySell reports 2,516 restaurant sales in 2025, a median 181 days on market, and a $215,000 median sale price, demonstrating active but predominantly small-business transaction flow.

sources.title: BizBuySell Restaurant Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Restaurant businesses sold from 2021 through 2025 had median reported revenue of $718,271, showing the marketplace evidence skews far below the target EBITDA band.

sources.title: National Restaurant Association State of the Restaurant Industry 2026 Executive Summary
sources.supports: Forecasts 1.3% real restaurant sales growth in 2026; reports 61% of adults view dining out as essential and 42% of operators were not profitable in 2025; notes persistent costs, soft traffic, and technology investment.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-34
sources.supports: For NAICS 722511, BLS projects employment from 5.3851 million in 2024 to 5.5177 million in 2034 and real output from $452.7 billion to $550.2 billion chained 2017 dollars, a 2.0% annual output growth rate.
